coredata.dc:title: Solid-state fluorescence from bioplastic nanocomposite embedded with intentional zinc doped carbon quantum dots synthesized from Brassica juncea flower extract for applications in UV-tube down-conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Varun Dutt
Kansay, Vishal
Bhatia, Anita
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: The enhancement of solid-state fluorescence from nanocomposite bioplastics containing intentionally Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been reported. For the synthesis of CQDs from Brassica juncea flower extract, an economical, ecologically sustainable, and one-step hydrothermal method was used. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical properties were examined. The quasi-circular shaped CQDs, with an average diameter of 4.7 nm, were discovered to emit yellow-greenish fluorescence upon UV stimulation, to have exceptional photostability, and to be highly water soluble, with a quantum yield of up to 18%. Furthermore, a commercial UVC-tube wrapped with bioplastic nanocomposite emitted a greenish-yellow colour where intentionally Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite emits more intensely than an unintentional one with CIE chromatic coordinates (0.39, 0.56 vs. 0.29, 0.55).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Bioplastic
UVC tube
Down-conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Brassica juncea-derived carbon quantum dots
Applications of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca, Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite for down-conversion LEDs and UV tubes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)-(c) HRTEM images, d-spacing from HR-TEM images, (d) Lorentzian-fitted particle size distribution histogram, (e) and EDX profile of intentionally Zn-doped CQDs derived from Brassica juncea flower extract.

(a) X-ray diffraction pattern and (b) FT-IR spectra of as-synthesized Zn-doped CQDs. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra (d) A comparison of FL spectra obtained from CQDs synthesised with and without the addition of Zinc. (e) After being exposed to UV light, aqueous suspended CQDs emit a bright and vivid green fluorescence.

Digital photographs of a commercially available UVC tube (254 nm, 11 W) (a) during OFF and ON conditions, (b) wrapped with bioplastic film only, (c) unintentionally N, Ca-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite, and (d) intentionally Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic composite, along with electroluminescent and CIE spectrum.

head.ce:title: Solid-state fluorescence from bioplastic nanocomposite embedded with intentional zinc doped carbon quantum dots synthesized from Brassica juncea flower extract for applications in UV-tube down-conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, MMEC, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala 133207, Haryana, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, MMEC, Maharishi Markandeshwar (Deemed to be University), Mullana, Ambala 133207, Haryana, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, DIT University, Uttarakhand, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, DIT University, Uttarakhand, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The enhancement of solid-state fluorescence from nanocomposite bioplastics containing intentionally Zn-doped carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been reported. For the synthesis of CQDs from Brassica juncea flower extract, an economical, ecologically sustainable, and one-step hydrothermal method was used. The morphological, structural, and physicochemical properties were examined. The quasi-circular shaped CQDs, with an average diameter of 4.7 nm, were discovered to emit yellow-greenish fluorescence upon UV stimulation, to have exceptional photostability, and to be highly water soluble, with a quantum yield of up to 18%. Furthermore, a commercial UVC-tube wrapped with bioplastic nanocomposite emitted a greenish-yellow colour where intentionally Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite emits more intensely than an unintentional one with CIE chromatic coordinates (0.39, 0.56 vs. 0.29, 0.55).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of wavelength down-conversion is an important economic strategy in LED lighting technology, in which high-energy or shorter wavelength light from near-UV is absorbed by down conversion materials and then re-emitted as lower energy or shorter wavelength visible light. The LED lighting business is expected to be valued USD 124.7 billion by 2027, with a compound annual growth rate of 10.6% [1–3]. CQDs are gaining significance in this respect as an inexpensive, environmentally sustainable luminescent material. CQDs commonly display excitation-dependent fluorescence due to surface states or defects generated by various doping components [4,5]. Furthermore, the development of biodegradable materials as a solid phase dispersion matrix of CQDs is crucial nowadays due to the severe environmental repercussions of synthetic plastics [6,7]. Hence, this study reports on the facile green synthesis of CQDs from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract with intentional Zn-doped and its influence on solid-state fluorescence while embedded in corn-starch-based nanocomposite bioplastic.

Mustard flower (scientific name–Brassica juncea, botanical family– Brassicaceae) as a precursor for CQDs was taken from the nearby agricultural field of the University campus (GPS locations: 30o25’ N, 77° 04′E) [8]. Analytical grade chemicals (acetone, ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, polyethylene glycol, and zinc acetate) were used as received without any further purifications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Brassica juncea-derived carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Brassica juncea flower extract was prepared by crushing it in a domestic mixer grinder and filtered. Thereafter two separate hydrothermal processes were performed, first, the extract mixed with polyethylene glycol was placed alone (sample #1), and then it was combined with zinc acetate (8:2 v/v ratio) to intentionally dope with Zn (sample #2) in a 50 ml Teflon-liner before being sonicated for 10 min. The hydrothermal process temperature was optimized to be 180 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature overnight, each as-prepared solution was centrifuged at 8000–9,000 rpm (7510–9500 g) for about 15 min. Following that, the solution was freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C after filtration and stored for future use. The as-synthesized material's pH value was estimated to be around 4.17. The relative quantum yield in reference to quinine sulphate was determined to be about 18%.

A biodegradable bioplastic was fabricated by combining 200 mg of corn starch in a mixture of glycerine and vinegar (1:1 v/v) for 10–15 min on a hotplate set to 50 °C. Following that, various concentrations of either sample #1 or 2 (ranging from 5 to 80 mg/ml) were added dropwise and constantly mixed for another 5–10 min. Using a doctored blade process, a thin film of CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposites (containing samples #1 and 2) was then coated on a glass slide. The nanocomposite films were then wrapped in a commercial UVC-tube light (254 nm, 11 W) commonly used as a RO UV filter for water purification or germ sterilisation and allowed to rest at room temperature for 24–48 h.

High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used to examine the morphological properties of intentionally Zn-doped CQDs (sample #2) at different scales, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a)-(b). Furthermore, lattice spacing (d-spacing) was calculated using rapid Fourier transformation, providing values ranging from 0.21 to 0.24 nm in relation to the graphite (002) plane, as shown in Fig. 1(c) [9,10]. The CQDs were nearly circular in shape and dispersed between 2 and 6 nm, with an average diameter of 4.7 nm as illustrated in the histogram shown in Fig. 1(d), indicating that CQDs have a significant quantum confinement effect.

EDX analysis of sample #2 shows that CQDs are effectively doped with Zn, N, and Ca at atomic percentages of 1.43, 2.8, and 2.88, respectively, and are thus denoted here as Zn, N,Ca-doped CQDs (see Fig. 1(e)). Similar compositions were also evident in the XPS spectra given in Fig. S1 and Table S1. XRD analysis, on the other hand, revels poor crystallinity, with a wide diffraction peak at 23.8° corresponds to (002) graphitic plane, as well as a few faint diffraction peaks at 42.2°, 43.9°, and 53.1° from the C (110), Ca (220), and Zn (224) planes, respectively (JCPDS Card No. 00–032-1987). The presence of numerous hydrophilic functional groups were confirmed by FTIR measurements in Fig. 2 (b) at stretching frequencies of 3421.72, 2920.23, 2123.63, 1656.25, 1460.11,1099.43, 939.33 and 457.87 cm−1, which clearly indicate the presence of –OH, -C-H, C-N, C = O, -Ca, and –Zn respectively. [11,12].

The outstanding optical characteristics have been demonstrated by optical absorption and emission investigations. It was observed that for intentionally Zn-doped CQDs, the prominent optical absorption peak red shifted from 324.6 nm to 392.6 nm and the bandgap was estimated to be 3.02 eV, as can be evidenced in Fig. 2(c) and supplementary Fig. S2. However, when excited at 280 nm, the fluorescence spectra of intentionally Zn-doped CQDs were found to emit more intensely than the unintentional one, as shown in Fig. 2 (d). Under normal light, the aqueous solutions of both CQDs were yellowish, but when exposed to UVC light, they fluoresced brightly in greenish colour as can be shown from Fig. 2(e). Besides, excitation dependent fluoresce was also observed (see Fig. S3 for details).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of Brassica juncea derived N, Ca, Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite for down-conversion LEDs and UV tubes
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, for large-scale applications, thin films of Zn-doped CQD@bioplastic nanocomposites were studied first, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The nanocomposite bioplastic film turns green when exposed to UV light, whereas the bioplastic film does not.

In addition, a commercial UVC tube, typically used for bacterial disinfection or RO water treatment, was chosen for large-scale applications of the fabricated eco-sustainable bioplastic nanocomposite to extend its properties as a visible tube light beyond its conventional use. When the UVC tube was wrapped with bioplastic film only, no changes in emitted colour were observed as can be seen from Fig. 3(b). However, when the tube was wrapped with bioplastic nanocomposite a greenish-yellow colour was emitted where intentionally Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite emits stronger than that of unintentional one as depicted in Figs. 3(c) and (d). This is also demonstrated by the corresponding electroluminescence spectra and Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE-1931) chromatic coordinates (0.39, 0.56 vs. 0.29, 0.55). Hence, this demonstration clearly shows how intentional Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite can improve the down-conversion efficiency of UV tubes. Future research may, however, concentrate on the improvement of fundamental optical properties (such as CIE and efficiency) of CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite.

In summary, an economical and green synthesis of CQDs derived from mustard (Brassica juncea) flower extract are reported. Because of the presence of minerals in the flower extracts, the as-synthesized CQDs were discovered to be unintentionally doped with nitrogen and calcium without the addition of doping element sources. However, the effect of intentional Zn-doping on fluorescence characteristics has been explored extensively. Besides, the morphological, structural, and physicochemical features of the material were investigated. The quasi-circular CQDs with an average diameter of 4.7 nm, excellent water solubility, and high quantum yield (∼18%) demonstrated yellow-greenish fluorescence in response to UV stimulation. A commercial UVC-tube wrapped in bioplastic nanocomposite released a greenish-yellow colour, whereas intentionally Zn-doped CQDs@bioplastic nanocomposite emits more intensely than an unintentional one with CIE chromatic coordinates (0.39, 0.56 vs. 0.29, 0.55).

Varun Dutt Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Vishal Kansay: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anita Bhatia: Visualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Supervision. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134629.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots/ZnO quantum dots composite-based white phosphors for white light-emitting diodes
Chem. Commun.

Chem. Soc. Rev.

J. Mater. Chem. C.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Kalaiyarasan, J. Joseph, Efficient dual-mode colorimetric/fluorometric sensor for the detection of copper ions and vitamin C based on pH-sensitive amino-terminated nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: effect of reactive oxygen species and antioxidants, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 411 (2019) 2619–2633. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00216-019-01710-8.
